summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of 'dogfooding' - using your own products - through the dramatic example of SawStop inventor Stephen Gass, who tested his table saw safety technology on his own finger. The post traces dogfooding's origins from Alpo commercials to Microsoft's internal adoption practices, then pivots to Gass's invention that can detect human tissue and stop a spinning blade in 1/100th of a second. Atwood argues that while dogfooding isn't always possible in software, developers should seek every opportunity to use their own products. The piece concludes with a pointed observation about Linux desktop developers who refuse to give up their terminals, suggesting this explains the state of Linux desktop software.
key_points: Dogfooding originated with Alpo dog food commercials and was popularized at Microsoft in 1988 when managers challenged teams to increase internal product usage
SawStop technology combines capacitance sensing to detect human tissue with a mechanism that stops blades in under 1/100th of a second
The inventor testing his own finger on a spinning blade represents the ultimate expression of product confidence
Even with safety technology, physics imposes limits - the saw still cuts about 1/16 inch per foot-per-second of movement
Linux desktop developers who won't give up terminals exemplify the failure to dogfood, which explains poor desktop Linux usability
money_quotes: Nothing exudes confidence like software developers willing to stick their own extremities into the spinning blades of software they've written.
This is a guy who really wanted to close the distance between him and his customers.
No matter how much I believed in my product, I think I would find it incredibly difficult to stick my finger in a spinning table saw blade.

It would be wonderful discipline for any software dev team serious about Linux 'on the desktop' to ban their own use of terminals. Of course, none of us have ever done this, and that explains a lot about the resulting products.
A table saw is an extremely dangerous tool... It should be obvious what would happen if a finger ever came in contact with the spinning blade.
This saw cuts you about 1/16 inch for every foot per second that you're moving.
key_insight: The willingness to personally depend on your own product - to have real skin in the game - is the truest test of confidence in what you've built and the surest path to building something worth using.
